AI Overview
Coffee’s journey began in 9th-century Ethiopia, where legend claims a goat herder named Kaldi discovered the energizing beans. The brew was later popularized by Sufi monks in 15th-century Yemen to stay awake for prayers, eventually evolving into the global commodity and cultural staple enjoyed worldwide today. 
About Coffee - National Coffee Association

Origins
The coffee in your cup starts on a tree! Coffee “beans” are the seeds of the coffee tree, harvested and roasted from coffee-growing regions across the world. This is coffee’s origin story.

Here you’ll find information about coffee and health, all based on independent research, including evidence that shows that drinking coffee is linked to overall longer, healthier lives.

AI Overview
Coffee’s journey began in 9th-century Ethiopia, where legend claims a goat herder named Kaldi discovered the energizing beans. The brew was later popularized by Sufi monks in 15th-century Yemen to stay awake for prayers, eventually evolving into the global commodity and cultural staple enjoyed worldwide today. 
About Coffee - National Coffee Association
 +1
Tracing the timeline of coffee reveals how it transformed from a regional secret into an everyday ritual: 
Ancient Origins (Ethiopia): Early tribes in the Horn of Africa ground coffee cherries into a paste with animal fat for energy before battle.
The Arabian Spread (15th–16th Centuries): Cultivation and trade thrived on the Arabian Peninsula. Sufi monks in Yemen brewed the roasted beans, coining the term qahwah (which originally referred to wine). This era also saw the birth of the first qahveh khaneh (coffeehouses) in Mecca and Constantinople.
European Arrival (17th Century): Travelers brought coffee to Europe, where it sparked a cultural revolution. It arrived in Italy in 1570, leading to the opening of Europe's oldest continuously operating coffee house, the legendary Caffè Florian in Venice, in 1720.
Global Expansion (17th–18th Centuries): Seeking to break the Arabian monopoly, the Dutch began cultivating the plant in Sri Lanka and Java. The plant was eventually smuggled and planted across the Americas, turning Brazil into the world’s largest producer.
The Espresso Revolution (20th Century): Italy heavily shaped modern coffee consumption. The first espresso machine was patented in 1884, and engineers refined the process, bringing us the modern espresso in the 1930s.
The Specialty Era (1970s–Present): The rise of specialty coffee chains, beginning with the opening of the first Starbucks in Seattle in 1971, popularized artisanal roasting, single-origin beans, and a deeper appreciation for the beverage's terroir. 
About Coffee - National Coffee Association
